questions.question: Explain the difference between KGDB and KDB kernel debuggers.
keyPoints: KGDB allows remote debugging using gdb over serial/Ethernet connections
KDB is a built-in lightweight debugger accessible directly on the target machine
KGDB supports full gdb features like breakpoints and stepping
KDB provides immediate access without requiring a separate debugging host

questions.question: What are the four necessary conditions for deadlock? Explain each briefly.
keyPoints: Mutual Exclusion: Only one process can use a resource at a time
Hold and Wait: Process holds resources while waiting for others
No Preemption: Resources cannot be forcibly taken from processes
Circular Wait: Circular chain of processes each waiting for resource held by next

questions.question: Describe the purpose and functioning of printk() in Linux kernel debugging.
keyPoints: printk() is the kernel equivalent of printf for logging messages
Supports multiple log levels from KERN_EMERG (0) to KERN_DEBUG (7)
Messages are written to kernel log buffer accessible via dmesg
Works at all privilege levels including interrupt context

questions.question: What is ftrace and what are its main capabilities?
keyPoints: ftrace is a built-in function tracer in the Linux kernel
Provides function graph tracing showing call relationships
Supports event tracing for kernel subsystems
Uses debugfs interface for control and output

questions.question: Explain the concept of core dump and its role in debugging.
keyPoints: Core dump is a file containing memory image of a crashed process
Includes text, data, heap, stack segments, and register states
Enables post-mortem analysis without interrupting the system
Generated automatically on crash or programmatically via abort()

questions.question: What are the challenges unique to operating system debugging compared to application debugging?
keyPoints: OS runs in privileged mode with direct hardware access
Errors can cause complete system failure rather than single process crash
Cannot use standard interactive debuggers safely
Limited visibility into kernel state during execution

questions.question: Describe in detail the tools and techniques used for debugging the Linux kernel. Include at least five different approaches with their specific use cases.
keyPoints: printk(): Basic logging for kernel messages with different log levels
KGDB: Remote debugging using gdb over serial/Ethernet connections
KDB: Built-in interactive kernel debugger for live examination
ftrace: Function tracing and performance analysis
strace: Userspace system call tracing
perf: Performance profiling with hardware counters
debugfs: Accessing kernel internal data structures
SystemTap: Dynamic kernel instrumentation with custom probes
Crash utility: Analyzing kernel crash dumps

questions.question: Explain the concept of deadlock in operating systems. Discuss deadlock prevention, avoidance, and detection techniques with examples.
keyPoints: Deadlock definition: processes waiting indefinitely for resources held by each other
Four necessary conditions with explanation
Prevention: Eliminate one condition (e.g., preempt resources, prevent hold-and-wait)
Avoidance: Banker's algorithm for safe resource allocation
Detection: Resource allocation graph and periodic system checks
Recovery: Process termination or resource preemption

questions.question: Discuss the methods for analyzing kernel crashes and performing post-mortem debugging in Linux systems.
keyPoints: Kernel panic: Causes and automatic crash dump generation
Core dump configuration using ulimit and sysctl
Crash utility for analyzing vmcore files
Using kgdb for live kernel debugging with crash dump
Analyzing dmesg output for kernel messages
Using objdump and gdb for kernel image analysis
Interpreting stack traces to identify failure points

questions.question: Explain race conditions in operating systems. How do they occur and what techniques are used to prevent them?
keyPoints: Race condition definition: Unpredictable behavior due to timing of concurrent operations
Occurs when multiple threads access shared resources without synchronization
Critical section concept and mutual exclusion requirement
Prevention techniques: Locks, mutexes, semaphores, atomic operations
Hardware support: Test-and-set, compare-and-swap instructions
Software solutions: Peterson's algorithm, synchronization primitives
